quick_facts.label: Credential and regulator
quick_facts.value: B.Arch is the five-year undergraduate architecture qualification regulated through the Council of Architecture (CoA) standards framework; the CoA oversees standards at approved architectural institutions.

quick_facts.label: Normal duration
quick_facts.value: The CoA minimum standard is 5 academic years (10 semesters), including one semester—about 16 working weeks—of practical training during semester 8 or 9.

quick_facts.label: Current entry eligibility
quick_facts.value: For 2026–27 admission, CoA’s NATA brochure states that applicants need 10+2/equivalent with Physics and Mathematics compulsory plus one listed subject (including Chemistry, Biology, Computer Science, IT, Engineering Graphics or Business Studies), with at least 45% aggregate; or a 10+3 Diploma with compulsory Mathematics and at least 45% aggregate.

quick_facts.label: Admission tests
quick_facts.value: Aptitude-test qualification is required for B.Arch admission; CoA identifies NATA or NTA’s JEE as the relevant tests. Actual seat allocation and additional conditions are set by the applicable state/UT counselling authority or institution.

quick_facts.label: Professional registration outcome
quick_facts.value: A graduate holding a recognised architectural qualification may apply for entry in the Register of Architects under section 25 of the Architects Act, 1972. The statutory title “architect” is reserved for registered architects or firms of architects.

sections.heading: Eligibility: school subjects and qualifying route
paragraphs: For the 2026–27 B.Arch admission cycle, the CoA-published eligibility route is not limited to the traditional PCM combination: candidates must have passed (or be appearing in) 10+2/equivalent with Physics and Mathematics as compulsory subjects, plus one approved third subject—Chemistry, Biology, a Technical Vocational subject, Computer Science, Information Technology, Informatics Practices, Engineering Graphics or Business Studies—and at least 45% aggregate marks. A 10+3 Diploma with Mathematics compulsory and at least 45% aggregate is the alternate academic route.

Meeting the academic-subject rule alone is insufficient. Candidates also need the prescribed architecture aptitude-test qualification, and the relevant admission authority or institution may apply its own counselling, reservation and document-verification rules. Requirements should therefore be checked against the specific institution and state counselling notice in the year of application.
bullets: The stated 45% threshold and permitted subject combinations are current 2026–27 context and may be amended in a later admission cycle.
NATA eligibility to sit the test does not itself guarantee a B.Arch seat or admission.
Reservation policy is governed by the relevant central, state or UT authority rather than by NATA alone.

sections.heading: Duration and practical training
paragraphs: The regulated B.Arch structure has a minimum duration of five academic years, organised as ten semesters of 15–18 working weeks each. It includes approximately 16 working weeks (six months or one semester) of practical training in the eighth or ninth semester. Students must complete that practical training or internship before enrolling for the tenth-semester architectural-design thesis, dissertation or project.

The 2020 regulations permit a maximum completion period of eight years, with a possible one-time additional year in special circumstances at the university or institution’s discretion. They also allow an institution to provide an exit option after a student has completed and earned the credits for the first three years; this is an institutional option, not a substitute for completing B.Arch.
bullets: Minimum programme length: 5 academic years / 10 semesters.
Required practical training: one semester, normally in semester 8 or 9.
Final design thesis/dissertation/project follows completion of training or internship.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: CoA’s Choice Based Credit System framework assigns 50% of curriculum credits to professional core courses and 20% to building sciences and applied engineering. The remaining framework provides for professional and open electives, professional-ability enhancement, and skill-enhancement courses; universities can vary nomenclature and emphasis within the regulatory framework.

Core learning spans design and visual arts, architectural design and thesis work, graphics and drawing, history and theory, urban design, settlements planning, housing, landscape and site planning, and cost estimation. Building-science and engineering study includes materials, construction, mechanics, structures, climatology, building services, surveying, acoustics and environmental subjects. The standards also identify professional practice, project management, internship/practical training, research or seminar work, computer studio, BIM and digital graphics among the curriculum components.

Teaching is studio- and project-intensive alongside lectures, seminars, labs, workshops, internships and study tours. The CoA framework describes internships in architectural practices or government architecture departments under CoA-registered architects.
bullets: Indicative compulsory professional elements include architectural design, construction, structures, building services and professional practice.
The specific semester-by-semester syllabus, assessment pattern and elective availability vary by university or institution.
Study tours and visits to places of architectural interest are an integral curricular expectation.

sections.heading: Admission: NATA, JEE Main and counselling
paragraphs: For B.Arch, CoA states that the required aptitude test may be conducted by the Council of Architecture as NATA or by the National Testing Agency as JEE. NATA is a qualifying aptitude test; the actual admission process is conducted by the relevant state/UT authority or institution using valid test scores and the applicable eligibility rules.

In the 2026 cycle, NATA’s first phase ran from April 4, 2026 through June 13, 2026, with a further August 7–8, 2026 phase described for vacant institutional seats after centralised admission rounds. These dates are cycle-specific rather than a recurring annual timetable. JEE Main lists Paper 2A for B.Arch separately from Paper 2B for B.Planning; candidates should choose the paper that matches the programme sought and follow the current NTA information bulletin and the target institute’s counselling rules.
bullets: NATA: commonly used for CoA-approved architecture institutions, subject to the relevant authority’s counselling rules.
JEE Main Paper 2A: the B.Arch paper; it is distinct from Paper 2B (B.Planning).
A score does not override the B.Arch subject-and-marks eligibility requirement or institute-specific admission conditions.

sections.heading: After B.Arch: registration and career direction
paragraphs: The principal regulated professional route is to complete a recognised architectural qualification and apply to the CoA for registration. Under the Architects Act, an “architect” is a person whose name is entered in the register, and section 37 prohibits anyone other than a registered architect or firm of architects from using the title and style of “architect.” A B.Arch graduate should verify that the exact awarding qualification and institution are recognised for registration, rather than assuming that every architecture-labelled course confers that outcome.

The degree also builds a foundation for employment and later specialisation in architectural practice, government architecture departments, construction and project roles, urban-design or planning-adjacent work, interiors, sustainability, conservation and design technology. The precise role, employer requirements and any postgraduate admission rules vary; registration is the key statutory step for using the protected architect title in India.
bullets: Apply for CoA registration after obtaining a recognised qualification and meeting the application-document requirements.
Keep semester records, final/provisional degree documentation and practical-training evidence available for the registration process.
For postgraduate or adjacent-field routes such as M.Arch, M.Plan, M.Des, MBA or GATE-based opportunities, check the current programme or examination eligibility separately.
